g.data-narration: Welcome to understanding Distributed Consensus - the foundation of blockchain technology that enables decentralized agreement.
Consensus is how multiple nodes agree on a single data value or state without a central authority controlling the network.
g.text: A process by which multiple nodes agree on a single data value or state, even when
some nodes fail or behave maliciously. Foundational for blockchain functionality.

g.data-narration: In decentralized networks, multiple nodes maintain copies of data and may receive transactions in different orders.
g.text: In decentralized networks with no central authority:
Multiple nodes maintain copies of the same data
Nodes may receive transactions in different orders
Some nodes may be offline, slow, or malicious

g.data-narration: The core consensus problem involves three key questions that distributed systems must answer about transactions.
g.text: Network of computers must agree on:

g.data-narration: Visualize a network where multiple nodes propose blocks and must reach agreement on which block to add to the chain.
The first two properties: Agreement ensures all honest nodes decide the same value, while Validity ensures the output reflects actual proposals.
Termination requires all nodes eventually reach a decision. Integrity ensures nodes only decide on values that were actually proposed.
Consensus must handle two failure types: Crash faults where nodes stop working, and Byzantine faults where nodes behave maliciously.
g.text: Types of Failures Consensus Must Handle
network disconnect). Don't send malicious messages.
Nodes behave arbitrarily - sending wrong data,
lying, colluding. Named after Byzantine Generals Problem.

g.data-narration: Blockchains navigate CAP theorem trade-offs between Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.
g.text: P, choosing between C and A

g.data-narration: Traditional databases rely on central authority, while blockchain uses distributed, trustless consensus mechanisms.
Permissioned blockchains use efficient voting protocols with known participants, while permissionless requires Sybil-resistant mechanisms.
Consensus enables cryptocurrency networks, supply chain tracking, healthcare records, voting systems, and IoT networks.
After mastering consensus, you'll be ready to explore Byzantine Fault Tolerance, Proof of Work, Proof of Stake, and PBFT.
Remember the four key properties: Agreement, Validity, Termination, and Integrity - the foundation of all consensus mechanisms.
Consensus is the foundation enabling decentralized nodes to agree on truth while handling both crash and Byzantine faults.
g.text: • Consensus allows decentralized nodes to agree on a single truth
• Must handle crash faults and Byzantine (malicious) faults
